12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54

73	    with client:

8	@pytest.fixture
9	def app():
10	    app = FastAPI()
11	    app.add_middleware(
12	        RateLimitMiddleware,
13	        requests_per_minute=60,
14	        burst_size=5,
15	    )
16	
17	    @app.get("/")
18	    async def root():
19	        return {"message": "hello"}
20	
21	    @app.get("/other")
22	    async def other():
23	        return {"message": "other"}

28	@pytest.fixture

33	class TestRateLimitMiddleware:
34	    def test_allows_requests_under_limit(self, client):
35	        """Requests under the burst size should succeed."""
36	        for _ in range(5):
37	            response = client.get("/")
38	            assert response.status_code == 200
39	
40	    def test_blocks_requests_over_burst(self, client):
41	        """Requests exceeding burst size should be blocked."""
42	        # Use up the burst
43	        for _ in range(5):
44	            client.get("/")
45	        # Next request should be rate limited
46	        response = client.get("/")
47	        assert response.status_code == 429
48	        body = response.json()
49	        assert "Rate limit exceeded" in body["detail"]
50	        assert "retry_after" in body
51	
52	    def test_rate_limit_headers_present(self, client):
53	        """All responses should include rate limit headers."""
54	        response = client.get("/")
55	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
56	        assert "X-RateLimit-Remaining" in response.headers
57	        assert "X-RateLimit-Reset" in response.headers
58	
59	    def test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases(self, client):
60	        """Remaining count should decrease with each request."""
61	        r1 = client.get("/")
62	        remaining1 = int(r1.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
63	        r2 = client.get("/")
64	        remaining2 = int(r2.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
65	        assert remaining2 < remaining1
66	
67	    def test_rate_limit_per_ip(self, client):
68	        """Rate limits should be per-client IP."""
69	        # TestClient always uses the same IP, so this tests the basic flow
70	        response = client.get("/")
71	        assert response.status_code == 200
72	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
73	        assert response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "60"
74	
75	    def test_different_paths_share_limit(self, client):
76	        """Rate limit applies across all paths for the same client."""
77	        # Use up burst across different endpoints
78	        for _ in range(3):
79	            client.get("/")
80	        for _ in range(2):
81	            client.get("/other")
82	        # Should be rate limited now
83	        response = client.get("/")
84	        assert response.status_code == 429
85	
86	    def test_429_response_format(self, client):
87	        """429 response should have proper JSON format."""
88	        for _ in range(5):
89	            client.get("/")
90	        response = client.get("/")
91	        assert response.status_code == 429
92	        body = response.json()
93	        assert isinstance(body["detail"], str)
94	        assert isinstance(body["retry_after"], (int, float))
95	        assert body["retry_after"] > 0
96	
97	    def test_custom_limits(self):
98	        """Should respect custom rate limit configuration."""
99	        app = FastAPI()
100	        app.add_middleware(
101	            RateLimitMiddleware,
102	            requests_per_minute=120,
103	            burst_size=2,
104	        )
105	
106	        @app.get("/")
107	        async def root():
108	            return {"ok": True}
109	
110	        client = TestClient(app)
111	        # Burst of 2
112	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
113	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 200
114	        assert client.get("/").status_code == 429

6	def test_cors():
7	    client = TestClient(app)
8	    # Test pre-flight response
9	    headers = {
10	        "Origin": "https://localhost.tiangolo.com",
11	        "Access-Control-Request-Method": "GET",
12	        "Access-Control-Request-Headers": "X-Example",
13	    }
14	    response = client.options("/", headers=headers)
15	    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
16	    assert response.text == "OK"
17	    assert (
18	        response.headers["access-control-allow-origin"]
19	        == "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"
20	    )
21	    assert response.headers["access-control-allow-headers"] == "X-Example"
22	
23	    # Test standard response
24	    headers = {"Origin": "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"}
25	    response = client.get("/", headers=headers)
26	    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
27	    assert response.json() == {"message": "Hello World"}
28	    assert (
29	        response.headers["access-control-allow-origin"]
30	        == "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"
31	    )
32	
33	    # Test non-CORS response
34	    response = client.get("/")
35	    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
36	    assert response.json() == {"message": "Hello World"}
37	    assert "access-control-allow-origin" not in response.headers

6	# Used mainly to close files after the request is done, dependencies are closed

8	class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
9	    def __init__(
10	        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
11	    ) -> None:
12	        self.app = app
13	        self.context_name = context_name
14	
15	    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
16	        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
17	            scope[self.context_name] = stack
18	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)

11	
12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54

1	# Middleware { #middleware }
2	
3	You can add middleware to **FastAPI** applications.
4	
5	A "middleware" is a function that works with every **request** before it is processed by any specific *path operation*. And also with every **response** before returning it.
6	
7	* It takes each **request** that comes to your application.
8	* It can then do something to that **request** or run any needed code.
9	* Then it passes the **request** to be processed by the rest of the application (by some *path operation*).
10	* It then takes the **response** generated by the application (by some *path operation*).
11	* It can do something to that **response** or run any needed code.
12	* Then it returns the **response**.
13	
14	/// note | Technical Details
15	
16	If you have dependencies with `yield`, the exit code will run *after* the middleware.
17	
18	If there were any background tasks (covered in the [Background Tasks](background-tasks.md) section, you will see it later), they will run *after* all the middleware.
19	
20	///
21	
22	## Create a middleware { #create-a-middleware }
23	
24	To create a middleware you use the decorator `@app.middleware("http")` on top of a function.
25	
26	The middleware function receives:
27	
28	* The `request`.
29	* A function `call_next` that will receive the `request` as a parameter.
30	    * This function will pass the `request` to the corresponding *path operation*.
31	    * Then it returns the `response` generated by the corresponding *path operation*.
32	* You can then further modify the `response` before returning it.
33	
34	{* ../../docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py hl[8:9,11,14] *}
35	
36	/// tip
37	
38	Keep in mind that custom proprietary headers can be added [using the `X-` prefix](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers).
39	
40	But if you have custom headers that you want a client in a browser to be able to see, you need to add them to your CORS configurations ([CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)](cors.md)) using the parameter `expose_headers` documented in [Starlette's CORS docs](https://www.starlette.dev/middleware/#corsmiddleware).
41	
42	///
43	
44	/// note | Technical Details
45	
46	You could also use `from starlette.requests import Request`.
47	
48	**FastAPI** provides it as a convenience for you, the developer. But it comes directly from Starlette.
49	
50	///
51	
52	### Before and after the `response` { #before-and-after-the-response }
53	
54	You can add code to be run with the `request`,  before any *path operation* receives it.
55	
56	And also after the `response` is generated, before returning it.
57	
58	For example, you could add a custom header `X-Process-Time` containing the time in seconds that it took to process the request and generate a response:
59	
60	{* ../../docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py hl[10,12:13] *}
61	
62	/// tip
63	
64	Here we use [`time.perf_counter()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/time.html#time.perf_counter) instead of `time.time()` because it can be more precise for these use cases. 🤓
65	
66	///
67	
68	## Multiple middleware execution order { #multiple-middleware-execution-order }
69	
70	When you add multiple middlewares using either `@app.middleware()` decorator or `app.add_middleware()` method, each new middleware wraps the application, forming a stack. The last middleware added is the *outermost*, and the first is the *innermost*.
71	
72	On the request path, the *outermost* middleware runs first.
73	
74	On the response path, it runs last.
75	
76	For example:
77	
78	```Python
79	app.add_middleware(MiddlewareA)
80	app.add_middleware(MiddlewareB)
81	```
82	
83	This results in the following execution order:
84	
85	* **Request**: MiddlewareB → MiddlewareA → route
86	
87	* **Response**: route → MiddlewareA → MiddlewareB
88	
89	This stacking behavior ensures that middlewares are executed in a predictable and controllable order.
90	
91	## Other middlewares { #other-middlewares }
92	
93	You can later read more about other middlewares in the [Advanced User Guide: Advanced Middleware](../advanced/middleware.md).
94	
95	You will read about how to handle <abbr title="Cross-Origin Resource Sharing">CORS</abbr> with a middleware in the next section.
96

1	# Advanced Middleware { #advanced-middleware }
2	
3	In the main tutorial you read how to add [Custom Middleware](../tutorial/middleware.md) to your application.
4	
5	And then you also read how to handle [CORS with the `CORSMiddleware`](../tutorial/cors.md).
6	
7	In this section we'll see how to use other middlewares.
8	
9	## Adding ASGI middlewares { #adding-asgi-middlewares }
10	
11	As **FastAPI** is based on Starlette and implements the <abbr title="Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface">ASGI</abbr> specification, you can use any ASGI middleware.
12	
13	A middleware doesn't have to be made for FastAPI or Starlette to work, as long as it follows the ASGI spec.
14	
15	In general, ASGI middlewares are classes that expect to receive an ASGI app as the first argument.
16	
17	So, in the documentation for third-party ASGI middlewares they will probably tell you to do something like:
18	
19	```Python
20	from unicorn import UnicornMiddleware
21	
22	app = SomeASGIApp()
23	
24	new_app = UnicornMiddleware(app, some_config="rainbow")
25	```
26	
27	But FastAPI (actually Starlette) provides a simpler way to do it that makes sure that the internal middlewares handle server errors and custom exception handlers work properly.
28	
29	For that, you use `app.add_middleware()` (as in the example for CORS).
30	
31	```Python
32	from fastapi import FastAPI
33	from unicorn import UnicornMiddleware
34	
35	app = FastAPI()
36	
37	app.add_middleware(UnicornMiddleware, some_config="rainbow")
38	```
39	
40	`app.add_middleware()` receives a middleware class as the first argument and any additional arguments to be passed to the middleware.
41	
42	## Integrated middlewares { #integrated-middlewares }
43	
44	**FastAPI** includes several middlewares for common use cases, we'll see next how to use them.
45	
46	/// note | Technical Details
47	
48	For the next examples, you could also use `from starlette.middleware.something import SomethingMiddleware`.
49	
50	**FastAPI** provides several middlewares in `fastapi.middleware` just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available middlewares come directly from Starlette.
51	
52	///
53	
54	## `HTTPSRedirectMiddleware` { #httpsredirectmiddleware }
55	
56	Enforces that all incoming requests must either be `https` or `wss`.
57	
58	Any incoming request to `http` or `ws` will be redirected to the secure scheme instead.
59	
60	{* ../../docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial001_py310.py hl[2,6] *}
61	
62	## `TrustedHostMiddleware` { #trustedhostmiddleware }
63	
64	Enforces that all incoming requests have a correctly set `Host` header, in order to guard against HTTP Host Header attacks.
65	
66	{* ../../docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial002_py310.py hl[2,6:8] *}
67	
68	The following arguments are supported:
69	
70	* `allowed_hosts` - A list of domain names that should be allowed as hostnames. Wildcard domains such as `*.example.com` are supported for matching subdomains. To allow any hostname either use `allowed_hosts=["*"]` or omit the middleware.
71	* `www_redirect` - If set to True, requests to non-www versions of the allowed hosts will be redirected to their www counterparts. Defaults to `True`.
72	
73	If an incoming request does not validate correctly then a `400` response will be sent.
74	
75	## `GZipMiddleware` { #gzipmiddleware }
76	
77	Handles GZip responses for any request that includes `"gzip"` in the `Accept-Encoding` header.
78	
79	The middleware will handle both standard and streaming responses.
80	
81	{* ../../docs_src/advanced_middleware/tutorial003_py310.py hl[2,6] *}
82	
83	The following arguments are supported:
84	
85	* `minimum_size` - Do not GZip responses that are smaller than this minimum size in bytes. Defaults to `500`.
86	* `compresslevel` - Used during GZip compression. It is an integer ranging from 1 to 9. Defaults to `9`. Lower value results in faster compression but larger file sizes, while higher value results in slower compression but smaller file sizes.
87	
88	## Other middlewares { #other-middlewares }
89	
90	There are many other ASGI middlewares.
91	
92	For example:
93	
94	* [Uvicorn's `ProxyHeadersMiddleware`](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/master/uvicorn/middleware/proxy_headers.py)
95	* [MessagePack](https://github.com/florimondmanca/msgpack-asgi)
96	
97	To see other available middlewares check [Starlette's Middleware docs](https://www.starlette.dev/middleware/) and the [ASGI Awesome List](https://github.com/florimondmanca/awesome-asgi).
98

3	from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, File, UploadFile
4	from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
5	from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
6	
7	app = FastAPI()
8	
9	router = APIRouter()

11	
12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46	
47	    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
54	
55	
56	@router.post("/middleware")
57	def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(..., description="Big File")):
58	    return {"message": "OK"}
59	
60	
61	app.include_router(router)
62	app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)
63	
64	
65	client = TestClient(app)
66

68	def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
69	    default_pydantic_max_size = 2**16
70	    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
71	    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (default_pydantic_max_size + 1))
72	
73	    with client:
74	        with open(path, "rb") as file:
75	            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
76	        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
77	        assert response.json() == {
78	            "detail": {
79	                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
80	                "code": 999,
81	                "message": "File limit exceeded",
82	            }
83	        }

229	                A list of tags used by OpenAPI, these are the same `tags` you can set

234	
235	                The order of the tags can be used to specify the order shown in
236	                tools like Swagger UI, used in the automatic path `/docs`.
237	
238	                It's not required to specify all the tags used.
239

242	
243	                The value of each item is a `dict` containing:

248	                    text representation.
249	                * `externalDocs`: Additional external documentation for this tag. If

4694	            return response
4695	        ```
4696	        """

1	# CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) { #cors-cross-origin-resource-sharing }
2	
3	[CORS or "Cross-Origin Resource Sharing"](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS) refers to the situations when a frontend running in a browser has JavaScript code that communicates with a backend, and the backend is in a different "origin" than the frontend.
4	
5	## Origin { #origin }
6	
7	An origin is the combination of protocol (`http`, `https`), domain (`myapp.com`, `localhost`, `localhost.tiangolo.com`), and port (`80`, `443`, `8080`).
8	
9	So, all these are different origins:
10	
11	* `http://localhost`
12	* `https://localhost`
13	* `http://localhost:8080`
14	
15	Even if they are all in `localhost`, they use different protocols or ports, so, they are different "origins".
16	
17	## Steps { #steps }
18	
19	So, let's say you have a frontend running in your browser at `http://localhost:8080`, and its JavaScript is trying to communicate with a backend running at `http://localhost` (because we don't specify a port, the browser will assume the default port `80`).
20	
21	Then, the browser will send an HTTP `OPTIONS` request to the `:80`-backend, and if the backend sends the appropriate headers authorizing the communication from this different origin (`http://localhost:8080`) then the `:8080`-browser will let the JavaScript in the frontend send its request to the `:80`-backend.
22	
23	To achieve this, the `:80`-backend must have a list of "allowed origins".
24	
25	In this case, the list would have to include `http://localhost:8080` for the `:8080`-frontend to work correctly.
26	
27	## Wildcards { #wildcards }
28	
29	It's also possible to declare the list as `"*"` (a "wildcard") to say that all are allowed.
30	
31	But that will only allow certain types of communication, excluding everything that involves credentials: Cookies, Authorization headers like those used with Bearer Tokens, etc.
32	
33	So, for everything to work correctly, it's better to specify explicitly the allowed origins.
34	
35	## Use `CORSMiddleware` { #use-corsmiddleware }
36	
37	You can configure it in your **FastAPI** application using the `CORSMiddleware`.
38	
39	* Import `CORSMiddleware`.
40	* Create a list of allowed origins (as strings).
41	* Add it as a "middleware" to your **FastAPI** application.
42	
43	You can also specify whether your backend allows:
44	
45	* Credentials (Authorization headers, Cookies, etc).
46	* Specific HTTP methods (`POST`, `PUT`) or all of them with the wildcard `"*"`.
47	* Specific HTTP headers or all of them with the wildcard `"*"`.
48	
49	{* ../../docs_src/cors/tutorial001_py310.py hl[2,6:11,13:19] *}
50	
51	
52	The default parameters used by the `CORSMiddleware` implementation are restrictive by default, so you'll need to explicitly enable particular origins, methods, or headers, in order for browsers to be permitted to use them in a Cross-Domain context.
53	
54	The following arguments are supported:
55	
56	* `allow_origins` - A list of origins that should be permitted to make cross-origin requests. E.g. `['https://example.org', 'https://www.example.org']`. You can use `['*']` to allow any origin.
57	* `allow_origin_regex` - A regex string to match against origins that should be permitted to make cross-origin requests. e.g. `'https://.*\.example\.org'`.
58	* `allow_methods` - A list of HTTP methods that should be allowed for cross-origin requests. Defaults to `['GET']`. You can use `['*']` to allow all standard methods.
59	* `allow_headers` - A list of HTTP request headers that should be supported for cross-origin requests. Defaults to `[]`. You can use `['*']` to allow all headers. The `Accept`, `Accept-Language`, `Content-Language` and `Content-Type` headers are always allowed for [simple CORS requests](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS#simple_requests).
60	* `allow_credentials` - Indicate that cookies should be supported for cross-origin requests. Defaults to `False`.
61	
62	    None of `allow_origins`, `allow_methods` and `allow_headers` can be set to `['*']` if `allow_credentials` is set to `True`. All of them must be [explicitly specified](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS#credentialed_requests_and_wildcards).
63	
64	* `expose_headers` - Indicate any response headers that should be made accessible to the browser. Defaults to `[]`.
65	* `max_age` - Sets a maximum time in seconds for browsers to cache CORS responses. Defaults to `600`.
66	
67	The middleware responds to two particular types of HTTP request...
68	
69	### CORS preflight requests { #cors-preflight-requests }
70	
71	These are any `OPTIONS` request with `Origin` and `Access-Control-Request-Method` headers.
72	
73	In this case the middleware will intercept the incoming request and respond with appropriate CORS headers, and either a `200` or `400` response for informational purposes.
74	
75	### Simple requests { #simple-requests }
76	
77	Any request with an `Origin` header. In this case the middleware will pass the request through as normal, but will include appropriate CORS headers on the response.
78	
79	## More info { #more-info }
80	
81	For more info about <abbr title="Cross-Origin Resource Sharing">CORS</abbr>, check the [Mozilla CORS documentation](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS).
82	
83	/// note | Technical Details
84	
85	You could also use `from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware`.
86	
87	**FastAPI** provides several middlewares in `fastapi.middleware` just as a convenience for you, the developer. But most of the available middlewares come directly from Starlette.
88	
89	///
90

Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile a comprehensive report:

## Summary: Starlette Middleware Base Classes in FastAPI

Based on my exploration of the FastAPI project, here's what I learned about how Starlette middleware works:

### 1. Starlette Installation
Starlette is **not vendored** in the FastAPI project - it's installed as an external dependency. The `pyproject.toml` specifies:
```
starlette>=0.46.0
```

### 2. Key Imports from Starlette (in `fastapi/applications.py`)
```python
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from starlette.middleware import Middleware
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.middleware.errors import ServerErrorMiddleware
from starlette.middleware.exceptions import ExceptionMiddleware
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, ExceptionHandler, Lifespan, Receive, Scope, Send
```

### 3. FastAPI Application Class Structure
- **Inherits from**: `Starlette` (line 45 of `fastapi/applications.py`)
- **Middleware Storage**: `self.user_middleware: list[Middleware]` (line 1016)
- **Middleware Stack**: `self.middleware_stack: ASGIApp | None` (line 1019)
- **Does NOT override `add_middleware()`** - it uses Starlette's inherited implementation

### 4. Middleware Dispatch Pattern (Build Stack Method)

FastAPI uses a sophisticated middleware ordering strategy via `build_middleware_stack()` (lines 1022-1070):

**Order (inside-out nesting)**:
1. **ServerErrorMiddleware** - catches unhandled server errors
2. **User Middleware** - custom middleware added via `app.add_middleware()`
3. **ExceptionMiddleware** - handles application exceptions
4. **AsyncExitStackMiddleware** - FastAPI-specific, for closing files and resources
5. **Router** - the actual route handler (core of the app)

This creates a middleware chain where requests flow through in this order:
```
ServerErrorMiddleware → User Middleware → ExceptionMiddleware → AsyncExitStackMiddleware → Router
```

### 5. Middleware Implementation Patterns

**Two patterns used in FastAPI**:

**Pattern 1: ASGI Raw Middleware** (AsyncExitStackMiddleware, ContentSizeLimitMiddleware)
```python
class CustomMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp):
        self.app = app
    
    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        # Process scope/receive/send
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

**Pattern 2: Starlette Re-exports** (CORSMiddleware, GZipMiddleware, HTTPSRedirectMiddleware)
- Located in `/fastapi/middleware/` directory
- Are simple re-exports from Starlette:
  ```python
  from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware as CORSMiddleware  # noqa
  ```

### 6. Type System Used
- **ASGIApp**: The ASGI application type
- **Scope/Receive/Send**: The ASGI protocol interface
- **Middleware**: Starlette's middleware wrapper class

### 7. Real Custom Middleware Examples in Tests

**`test_custom_middleware_exception.py`** shows a content-size limiting middleware:
```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
    
    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)

# Usage
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)
```

### 8. Expected Rate Limiting Middleware
Tests expect a `RateLimitMiddleware` to be created at `/fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py`:
- Constructor parameters: `requests_per_minute`, `burst_size`
- Response headers: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`
- Rate limit exceeded: Returns 429 with `{"detail": "...", "retry_after": <int>}`
- Tracking: Per-client IP, shared across all paths

### 9. Key Files and Locations

- **Main FastAPI class**: `/fastapi/applications.py` (41KB file)
- **Middleware directory**: `/fastapi/middleware/`
- **Test for rate limiter**: `/tests/test_rate_limiter.py` (expected implementation)
- **Custom middleware test**: `/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`
- **Middleware examples**: `/docs_src/advanced_middleware/`

### 10. Fastapi __init__.py Exports

FastAPI exports key types for public API:
```python
from starlette import status as status
from .applications import FastAPI as FastAPI
# ... routes, responses, exceptions, etc.
```

Note: The middleware module re-exports from Starlette but doesn't define new base classes.

1	import time
2	import pytest
3	from fastapi import FastAPI
4	from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
5	from fastapi.middleware.rate_limit import RateLimitMiddleware
6	
7	
8	@pytest.fixture
9	def app():
10	    app = FastAPI()
11	    app.add_middleware(
12	        RateLimitMiddleware,
13	        requests_per_minute=60,
14	        burst_size=5,
15	    )
16	
17	    @app.get("/")
18	    async def root():
19	        return {"message": "hello"}
20	
21	    @app.get("/other")
22	    async def other():
23	        return {"message": "other"}
24	
25	    return app
26	
27	
28	@pytest.fixture
29	def client(app):
30	    return TestClient(app)
31	
32	
33	class TestRateLimitMiddleware:
34	    def test_allows_requests_under_limit(self, client):
35	        """Requests under the burst size should succeed."""
36	        for _ in range(5):
37	            response = client.get("/")
38	            assert response.status_code == 200
39	
40	    def test_blocks_requests_over_burst(self, client):
41	        """Requests exceeding burst size should be blocked."""
42	        # Use up the burst
43	        for _ in range(5):
44	            client.get("/")
45	        # Next request should be rate limited
46	        response = client.get("/")
47	        assert response.status_code == 429
48	        body = response.json()
49	        assert "Rate limit exceeded" in body["detail"]
50	        assert "retry_after" in body
51	
52	    def test_rate_limit_headers_present(self, client):
53	        """All responses should include rate limit headers."""
54	        response = client.get("/")
55	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
56	        assert "X-RateLimit-Remaining" in response.headers
57	        assert "X-RateLimit-Reset" in response.headers
58	
59	    def test_rate_limit_remaining_decreases(self, client):
60	        """Remaining count should decrease with each request."""
61	        r1 = client.get("/")
62	        remaining1 = int(r1.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
63	        r2 = client.get("/")
64	        remaining2 = int(r2.headers["X-RateLimit-Remaining"])
65	        assert remaining2 < remaining1
66	
67	    def test_rate_limit_per_ip(self, client):
68	        """Rate limits should be per-client IP."""
69	        # TestClient always uses the same IP, so this tests the basic flow
70	        response = client.get("/")
71	        assert response.status_code == 200
72	        assert "X-RateLimit-Limit" in response.headers
73	        assert response.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "60"
74	
75	    def test_different_paths_share_limit(self, client):
76	        """Rate limit applies across all paths for the same client."""
77	        # Use up burst across different endpoints
78	        for _ in range(3):
79	            client.get("/")
80	        for _ in range(2):
81	            client.get("/other")
82	        # Should be rate limited now

84	        assert response.status_code == 429

Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the testing patterns. Let me create a summary document.

## FastAPI Testing Patterns - Comprehensive Report

Based on my exploration of the FastAPI project's test suite, here's a detailed analysis of the testing patterns used:

---

## 1. Directory Structure

The project uses a well-organized test structure:

```
tests/
├── __init__.py
├── main.py (shared test app)
├── test_application.py (main tests)
├── test_custom_middleware_exception.py
├── test_dependency_*.py (various dependency tests)
├── test_request_params/
│   ├── test_body/
│   ├── test_cookie/
│   ├── test_file/
│   ├── test_form/
│   ├── test_header/
│   ├── test_path/
│   └── test_query/
├── test_tutorial/
│   ├── test_cors/
│   ├── test_middleware/
│   ├── test_advanced_middleware/
│   ├── test_bigger_applications/
│   ├── test_stream_data/
│   ├── test_response_directly/
│   └── [20+ other tutorial-based tests]
├── test_modules_same_name_body/
└── benchmarks/

tests/test_tutorial/test_middleware/ includes:
  - test_tutorial001.py (basic middleware)

tests/test_tutorial/test_advanced_middleware/ includes:
  - test_tutorial001.py (HTTPS redirect)
  - test_tutorial002.py (Trusted host)
  - test_tutorial003.py (GZip compression)

tests/test_tutorial/test_cors/ includes:
  - test_tutorial001.py (CORS middleware)
```

---

## 2. Testing Framework & HTTP Client

**Framework**: pytest (version >= 9.0.0 based on pyproject.toml)

**HTTP Client**: FastAPI's built-in `TestClient` imported from `fastapi.testclient`

Key dependencies from `pyproject.toml`:
- `pytest >=9.0.0`
- `httpx >=0.23.0,<1.0.0` (underlying HTTP client library)
- `inline-snapshot >=0.21.1` (for snapshot testing)
- `pytest-xdist[psutil]>=2.5.0` (parallel testing)
- `pytest-cov>=4.0.0` (coverage)
- `pytest-sugar>=1.0.0` (better output)
- `pytest-timeout>=2.4.0`

**No conftest.py or pytest fixtures**: The project uses module-level TestClient instantiation instead of fixtures.

---

## 3. TestClient Setup Patterns

### Basic Setup (Most Common)
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_cors/test_tutorial001.py`

```python
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from docs_src.cors.tutorial001_py310 import app

client = TestClient(app)
```

Then use globally:
```python
def test_cors():
    response = client.options("/", headers=headers)
    assert response.status_code == 200
```

### Per-Test Instantiation (Alternative)
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_middleware/test_tutorial001.py`

```python
def test_middleware():
    client = TestClient(app, base_url="https://testserver")
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200
```

### Custom Base URL
Multiple TestClient instances with different `base_url` values in the same test:

File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_advanced_middleware/test_tutorial002.py`

```python
def test_middleware():
    client = TestClient(app, base_url="http://example.com")
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    
    client = TestClient(app, base_url="http://subdomain.example.com")
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200
```

### Context Manager Usage (for File Uploads)
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`

```python
with client:
    with open(path, "rb") as file:
        response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
```

---

## 4. HTTP Request Methods & Headers

**Standard HTTP methods**:
- `client.get(path)` - GET requests
- `client.post(path)` - POST requests
- `client.options(path)` - OPTIONS (for CORS preflight)

**Header passing**:
```python
headers = {
    "Origin": "https://localhost.tiangolo.com",
    "Access-Control-Request-Method": "GET",
    "Access-Control-Request-Headers": "X-Example",
}
response = client.options("/", headers=headers)
```

**File uploads**:
```python
response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
```

**Query strings**:
```python
response = client.get("/main-depends/?q=foo")
response = client.get("/main-depends/?q=foo&skip=100&limit=200")
```

**Special parameters**:
```python
response = client.get("/", follow_redirects=False)  # Don't follow redirects
response = client.get("/large", headers={"accept-encoding": "gzip"})
```

---

## 5. Assertion Patterns

### Status Code Assertions
```python
assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
assert response.status_code == 307, response.text
assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
```

### JSON Response Assertions
```python
assert response.json() == {"message": "Hello World"}
assert response.json() == {
    "detail": [
        {
            "type": "missing",
            "loc": ["query", "q"],
            "msg": "Field required",
            "input": None,
        }
    ]
}
```

### Text Content Assertions
```python
assert response.text == "OK"
assert "swagger-ui-dist" in response.text
```

### Header Assertions
```python
assert "X-Process-Time" in response.headers
assert response.headers["content-type"] == "text/html; charset=utf-8"
assert response.headers["access-control-allow-origin"] == "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"
assert "access-control-allow-origin" not in response.headers
assert int(response.headers["Content-Length"]) < 4000
assert response.headers["Content-Encoding"] == "gzip"
```

### Snapshot Testing
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_application.py`

```python
from inline_snapshot import snapshot

def test_openapi_schema():
    response = client.get("/openapi.json")
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.json() == snapshot({
        "openapi": "3.1.0",
        "info": {"title": "FastAPI", "version": "0.1.0"},
        # ... more details
    })
```

---

## 6. Middleware Testing Patterns

### Basic Middleware Test
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_middleware/test_tutorial001.py`

Tests that middleware adds response headers:
```python
def test_response_headers():
    response = client.get("/openapi.json")
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert "X-Process-Time" in response.headers
```

Corresponding middleware (from docs_src/middleware/tutorial001_py310.py):
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    return response
```

### CORS Middleware Test
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_cors/test_tutorial001.py`

Tests CORS preflight (OPTIONS) and regular requests:
```python
def test_cors():
    client = TestClient(app)
    
    # Test pre-flight response
    headers = {
        "Origin": "https://localhost.tiangolo.com",
        "Access-Control-Request-Method": "GET",
        "Access-Control-Request-Headers": "X-Example",
    }
    response = client.options("/", headers=headers)
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.text == "OK"
    assert response.headers["access-control-allow-origin"] == "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"
    assert response.headers["access-control-allow-headers"] == "X-Example"

    # Test standard response
    headers = {"Origin": "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"}
    response = client.get("/", headers=headers)
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.json() == {"message": "Hello World"}
    assert response.headers["access-control-allow-origin"] == "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"

    # Test non-CORS response (no Origin header)
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.json() == {"message": "Hello World"}
    assert "access-control-allow-origin" not in response.headers
```

Corresponding CORS middleware (from docs_src/cors/tutorial001_py310.py):
```python
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["https://localhost.tiangolo.com", ...],
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)
```

### Built-In Middleware Tests

**HTTPS Redirect**
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_advanced_middleware/test_tutorial001.py`

```python
def test_middleware():
    client = TestClient(app, base_url="https://testserver")
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text

    client = TestClient(app)
    response = client.get("/", follow_redirects=False)
    assert response.status_code == 307, response.text
    assert response.headers["location"] == "https://testserver/"
```

**Trusted Host**
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_advanced_middleware/test_tutorial002.py`

```python
def test_middleware():
    client = TestClient(app, base_url="http://example.com")
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    
    client = TestClient(app, base_url="http://subdomain.example.com")
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    
    client = TestClient(app, base_url="http://invalidhost")
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 400, response.text
```

**GZip Middleware**
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_advanced_middleware/test_tutorial003.py`

```python
def test_middleware():
    response = client.get("/large", headers={"accept-encoding": "gzip"})
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.text == "x" * 4000
    assert response.headers["Content-Encoding"] == "gzip"
    assert int(response.headers["Content-Length"]) < 4000
```

### Custom Middleware Test (with Exception Handling)
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`

Demonstrates custom ASGI middleware with exception raising:

```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0
        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message
            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(
                    422,
                    detail={
                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                        "code": 999,
                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
                    },
                )
            return message
        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)

# Register middleware
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)

# Test it
def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (2**16 + 1))

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
        assert response.json() == {
            "detail": {
                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                "code": 999,
                "message": "File limit exceeded",
            }
        }
```

---

## 7. Pytest Configuration

From `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/pyproject.toml`:

```toml
[tool.pytest]
minversion = "9.0"
addopts = [
  "--strict-config",
  "--strict-markers",
  "--ignore=docs_src",
]
strict_xfail = true
filterwarnings = [
    "error",  # Treat warnings as errors
]
timeout = "20"  # 20 second timeout per test
```

**Key features**:
- Strict configuration and marker validation
- Warnings treated as errors (strict testing)
- 20-second timeout per test
- docs_src ignored in test discovery

---

## 8. Test Parameterization

File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_application.py`

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "path,expected_status,expected_response",
    [
        ("/api_route", 200, {"message": "Hello World"}),
        ("/non_decorated_route", 200, {"message": "Hello World"}),
        ("/nonexistent", 404, {"detail": "Not Found"}),
    ],
)
def test_get_path(path, expected_status, expected_response):
    response = client.get(path)
    assert response.status_code == expected_status
    assert response.json() == expected_response
```

---

## 9. Key Testing Insights

1. **No pytest fixtures used** - The project consistently instantiates TestClient at module level or per-test
2. **Inline app definitions** - Many test files define the app directly in the test file for testing specific behaviors
3. **Snapshot testing** - Uses `inline-snapshot` for OpenAPI schema validation
4. **Header-driven testing** - Extensive testing of response headers, especially for middleware
5. **Parametrization** - Heavy use of `@pytest.mark.parametrize` for testing multiple scenarios
6. **File upload testing** - Uses context manager pattern and `tmp_path` fixture for temporary files
7. **Middleware-specific patterns**:
   - Testing preflight (OPTIONS) and regular (GET/POST) requests
   - Verifying response headers are added correctly
   - Testing redirect behavior with `follow_redirects=False`
   - Testing content encoding (gzip)
   - Custom base URLs for testing hostname/protocol requirements

---

## File Locations Reference

- **Main test app**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/main.py`
- **CORS tests**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_cors/test_tutorial001.py`
- **Middleware tests**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_middleware/test_tutorial001.py`
- **Advanced middleware tests**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_tutorial/test_advanced_middleware/test_tutorial00[1-3].py`
- **Custom middleware exception test**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`
- **Tutorial app sources**: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/docs_src/middleware/`, `/docs_src/cors/`, `/docs_src/advanced_middleware/`

    response = await call_next(request)

def test_cors():
    client = TestClient(app)
    
    # Test pre-flight response
    headers = {
        "Origin": "https://localhost.tiangolo.com",
        "Access-Control-Request-Method": "GET",
        "Access-Control-Request-Headers": "X-Example",
    }
    response = client.options("/", headers=headers)
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.text == "OK"
    assert response.headers["access-control-allow-origin"] == "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"
    assert response.headers["access-control-allow-headers"] == "X-Example"

    # Test standard response
    headers = {"Origin": "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"}
    response = client.get("/", headers=headers)
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.json() == {"message": "Hello World"}
    assert response.headers["access-control-allow-origin"] == "https://localhost.tiangolo.com"

    # Test non-CORS response (no Origin header)
    response = client.get("/")
    assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
    assert response.json() == {"message": "Hello World"}
    assert "access-control-allow-origin" not in response.headers
```

### Custom Middleware Test (with Exception Handling)
File: `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775390818544-zpc1zn/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`

Demonstrates custom ASGI middleware with exception raising:

```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0
        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message
            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(
                    422,
                    detail={
                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                        "code": 999,
                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
                    },
                )
            return message
        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)

# Register middleware
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)

# Test it
def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (2**16 + 1))

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
        assert response.json() == {
            "detail": {
                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                "code": 999,
                "message": "File limit exceeded",
            }
        }
```

## FastAPI Middleware Implementation Patterns - Comprehensive Analysis

Based on my thorough exploration of the FastAPI codebase, here's everything you need to know about how middleware is implemented and structured:

### 1. FastAPI Middleware Directory Structure

**Location:** `/fastapi/middleware/`

**Files:**
- `__init__.py` - Exports the `Middleware` class from Starlette
- `cors.py` - Re-exports `CORSMiddleware` from Starlette
- `gzip.py` - Re-exports `GZipMiddleware` from Starlette
- `httpsredirect.py` - Re-exports `HTTPSRedirectMiddleware` from Starlette
- `trustedhost.py` - Re-exports `TrustedHostMiddleware` from Starlette
- `wsgi.py` - Re-exports `WSGIMiddleware` from Starlette
- `asyncexitstack.py` - Custom FastAPI middleware for managing async context

### 2. How Middleware is Imported and Exported

From `/fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`:
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```

From individual middleware modules (example - `cors.py`):
```python
from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware as CORSMiddleware  # noqa
```

**Key Point:** FastAPI re-exports Starlette's middleware for developer convenience, but they are all imported from Starlette directly.

### 3. Base Starlette Middleware Classes Used

From `/fastapi/applications.py`, the key imports are:
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.middleware.errors import ServerErrorMiddleware
from starlette.middleware.exceptions import ExceptionMiddleware
```

**BaseHTTPMiddleware** is the primary base class used when creating custom middleware via the `@app.middleware("http")` decorator.

### 4. Custom AsyncExitStackMiddleware Pattern

**File:** `/fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py`

```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack"
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

**Key Pattern:**
- Class takes `app` as first parameter (ASGI app)
- Implements `__call__` as async method with signature: `(scope, receive, send)`
- Wraps the app execution and injects context into the scope dict

### 5. Middleware Stack Order and Execution

From `/fastapi/applications.py` (`build_middleware_stack` method, lines 1022-1070):

**Middleware Stack Structure:**
```python
middleware = (
    [Middleware(ServerErrorMiddleware, handler=error_handler, debug=debug)]
    + self.user_middleware
    + [
        Middleware(ExceptionMiddleware, handlers=exception_handlers, debug=debug),
        Middleware(AsyncExitStackMiddleware),
    ]
)

# Built in reverse order
app = self.router
for cls, args, kwargs in reversed(middleware):
    app = cls(app, *args, **kwargs)
```

**Execution Order:**
- **On Request:** ServerErrorMiddleware → (custom user middlewares) → ExceptionMiddleware → AsyncExitStackMiddleware → Router
- **On Response:** Router → AsyncExitStackMiddleware → ExceptionMiddleware → (custom user middlewares) → ServerErrorMiddleware

**Documented Order (from documentation):**
When you add middlewares:
```python
app.add_middleware(MiddlewareA)
app.add_middleware(MiddlewareB)
```

Request path: MiddlewareB → MiddlewareA → route  
Response path: route → MiddlewareA → MiddlewareB  
(Last added is outermost, first added is innermost)

### 6. Two Methods for Adding Middleware

#### Method 1: Decorator Approach - `@app.middleware("http")`

From `/fastapi/applications.py` (lines 4658-4702):

```python
def middleware(
    self,
    middleware_type: Annotated[str, Doc("The type of middleware. Currently only supports `http`.")],
) -> Callable[[DecoratedCallable], DecoratedCallable]:
    """
    Add a middleware to the application.
    """
    def decorator(func: DecoratedCallable) -> DecoratedCallable:
        self.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=func)
        return func
    
    return decorator
```

**Usage Example:**
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    return response
```

**Function Signature:**
- Takes `request` (Starlette Request)
- Takes `call_next` - a Callable that receives the request and returns an awaitable Response
- Must be async

#### Method 2: Class-Based Approach - `app.add_middleware()`

**Usage Example:**
```python
app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=origins, allow_credentials=True)
```

**Signature:** `add_middleware(cls, *args, **kwargs)` - inherited from Starlette

### 7. CORS Middleware Implementation Details

**File:** `/fastapi/middleware/cors.py`

From Starlette directly. Key usage:
```python
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["http://localhost", "https://localhost"],
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)
```

**Supported Parameters:**
- `allow_origins` - List of allowed origin domains (can use `["*"]` for wildcard, but not with credentials)
- `allow_origin_regex` - Regex pattern for allowed origins
- `allow_methods` - List of allowed HTTP methods (defaults to `["GET"]`)
- `allow_headers` - List of allowed request headers (defaults to `[]`)
- `allow_credentials` - Boolean, whether to allow cookies (defaults to `False`)
- `expose_headers` - List of response headers exposed to browser (defaults to `[]`)
- `max_age` - Cache time for CORS responses in seconds (defaults to `600`)

**How It Works:**
- Intercepts `OPTIONS` requests (CORS preflight) with `Origin` and `Access-Control-Request-Method` headers
- For simple requests with `Origin` header, passes through normal flow but adds CORS headers
- Returns 200/400 for preflight, normal response for simple requests

### 8. Custom Middleware Pattern - Content Size Limiting

From `/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`:

```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0
        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message
            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(422, detail={"message": "File limit exceeded"})
            return message
        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
```

**Usage:**
```python
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)
```

### 9. Key Type Signatures Used

From Starlette types (`starlette.types`):
```python
ASGIApp = Callable[[Scope, Receive, Send], Awaitable[None]]
Scope = dict[str, Any]
Receive = Callable[[], Awaitable[Message]]
Send = Callable[[Message], Awaitable[None]]
```

**ASGI Middleware Interface:**
```python
class Middleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        self.app = app
        # custom initialization with *args and **kwargs
    
    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        # middleware logic
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

### 10. Built-in Middlewares Available

From `/fastapi/middleware/`:
1. **CORSMiddleware** - CORS handling
2. **GZipMiddleware** - GZip compression for responses
3. **HTTPSRedirectMiddleware** - Redirect HTTP to HTTPS
4. **TrustedHostMiddleware** - Validate Host header
5. **WSGIMiddleware** - Wrap WSGI apps
6. **AsyncExitStackMiddleware** - FastAPI-specific context management

### 11. Key Documentation Files

- `/docs/en/docs/tutorial/middleware.md` - Basic middleware usage
- `/docs/en/docs/advanced/middleware.md` - Advanced patterns
- `/docs/en/docs/tutorial/cors.md` - CORS-specific documentation

### Summary of Critical Patterns

1. **FastAPI middlewares are thin wrappers** around Starlette implementations
2. **Middleware classes must implement** `async __call__(self, scope, receive, send)`
3. **Constructor pattern:** Takes `app` as first arg, then any configuration kwargs
4. **Two addition methods:** Decorator (`@app.middleware("http")`) or class (`app.add_middleware()`)
5. **Middleware stacking:** Last added = outermost (runs first on request, last on response)
6. **Custom stack:** ServerErrorMiddleware → CustomMiddleware → ExceptionMiddleware → AsyncExitStackMiddleware → Router
7. **Common modification points:** Request inspection, receive wrapping, send wrapping, adding headers

    """
    Add a middleware to the application.
    """
    def decorator(func: DecoratedCallable) -> DecoratedCallable:
        self.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=func)
        return func
    
    return decorator
```

**Usage Example:**
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    response = await call_next(request)
    process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
    return response
```

**Function Signature:**
- Takes `request` (Starlette Request)
- Takes `call_next` - a Callable that receives the request and returns an awaitable Response
- Must be async

#### Method 2: Class-Based Approach - `app.add_middleware()`

**Usage Example:**
```python
app.add_middleware(CORSMiddleware, allow_origins=origins, allow_credentials=True)
```

**Signature:** `add_middleware(cls, *args, **kwargs)` - inherited from Starlette

### 7. CORS Middleware Implementation Details

**File:** `/fastapi/middleware/cors.py`

From Starlette directly. Key usage:
```python
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["http://localhost", "https://localhost"],
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)
```

**Supported Parameters:**
- `allow_origins` - List of allowed origin domains (can use `["*"]` for wildcard, but not with credentials)
- `allow_origin_regex` - Regex pattern for allowed origins
- `allow_methods` - List of allowed HTTP methods (defaults to `["GET"]`)
- `allow_headers` - List of allowed request headers (defaults to `[]`)
- `allow_credentials` - Boolean, whether to allow cookies (defaults to `False`)
- `expose_headers` - List of response headers exposed to browser (defaults to `[]`)
- `max_age` - Cache time for CORS responses in seconds (defaults to `600`)

**How It Works:**
- Intercepts `OPTIONS` requests (CORS preflight) with `Origin` and `Access-Control-Request-Method` headers
- For simple requests with `Origin` header, passes through normal flow but adds CORS headers
- Returns 200/400 for preflight, normal response for simple requests

### 8. Custom Middleware Pattern - Content Size Limiting

From `/tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`:

```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0
        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message
            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(422, detail={"message": "File limit exceeded"})
            return message
        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
```

**Usage:**
```python
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)
```

### 9. Key Type Signatures Used

From Starlette types (`starlette.types`):
```python
ASGIApp = Callable[[Scope, Receive, Send], Awaitable[None]]
Scope = dict[str, Any]
Receive = Callable[[], Awaitable[Message]]
Send = Callable[[Message], Awaitable[None]]
```

**ASGI Middleware Interface:**
```python
class Middleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        self.app = app
        # custom initialization with *args and **kwargs
    
    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        # middleware logic
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

### 10. Built-in Middlewares Available

From `/fastapi/middleware/`:
1. **CORSMiddleware** - CORS handling
2. **GZipMiddleware** - GZip compression for responses
3. **HTTPSRedirectMiddleware** - Redirect HTTP to HTTPS
4. **TrustedHostMiddleware** - Validate Host header
5. **WSGIMiddleware** - Wrap WSGI apps
6. **AsyncExitStackMiddleware** - FastAPI-specific context management

### 11. Key Documentation Files

- `/docs/en/docs/tutorial/middleware.md` - Basic middleware usage
- `/docs/en/docs/advanced/middleware.md` - Advanced patterns
- `/docs/en/docs/tutorial/cors.md` - CORS-specific documentation

### Summary of Critical Patterns

1. **FastAPI middlewares are thin wrappers** around Starlette implementations
2. **Middleware classes must implement** `async __call__(self, scope, receive, send)`
3. **Constructor pattern:** Takes `app` as first arg, then any configuration kwargs
4. **Two addition methods:** Decorator (`@app.middleware("http")`) or class (`app.add_middleware()`)
5. **Middleware stacking:** Last added = outermost (runs first on request, last on response)
6. **Custom stack:** ServerErrorMiddleware → CustomMiddleware → ExceptionMiddleware → AsyncExitStackMiddleware → Router
7. **Common modification points:** Request inspection, receive wrapping, send wrapping, adding headers

from pathlib import Path

from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()

router = APIRouter()


class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
    Args:
      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
    """

    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
        received = 0

        async def inner():
            nonlocal received
            message = await receive()
            if message["type"] != "http.request":
                return message  # pragma: no cover

            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
            received += body_len
            if received > self.max_content_size:
                raise HTTPException(
                    422,
                    detail={
                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                        "code": 999,
                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
                    },
                )
            return message

        return inner

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return

        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)


@router.post("/middleware")
def run_middleware(file: UploadFile = File(..., description="Big File")):
    return {"message": "OK"}


app.include_router(router)
app.add_middleware(ContentSizeLimitMiddleware, max_content_size=2**8)


client = TestClient(app)


def test_custom_middleware_exception(tmp_path: Path):
    default_pydantic_max_size = 2**16
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"x" * (default_pydantic_max_size + 1))

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 422, response.text
        assert response.json() == {
            "detail": {
                "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
                "code": 999,
                "message": "File limit exceeded",
            }
        }


def test_custom_middleware_exception_not_raised(tmp_path: Path):
    path = tmp_path / "test.txt"
    path.write_bytes(b"<file content>")

    with client:
        with open(path, "rb") as file:
            response = client.post("/middleware", files={"file": file})
        assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
        assert response.json() == {"message": "OK"}

Design an implementation plan for adding a RateLimitMiddleware to FastAPI. Here's the full context:

## Codebase Context

### Middleware Directory: `fastapi/middleware/`
Files: `__init__.py`, `asyncexitstack.py`, `cors.py`, `gzip.py`, `httpsredirect.py`, `trustedhost.py`, `wsgi.py`

**`__init__.py`** contains only:
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware as Middleware
```

**`cors.py`** contains only:
```python
from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware as CORSMiddleware  # noqa
```

**`asyncexitstack.py`** is the only custom middleware in the codebase:
```python
from contextlib import AsyncExitStack
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send

class AsyncExitStackMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, context_name: str = "fastapi_middleware_astack") -> None:
        self.app = app
        self.context_name = context_name

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
            scope[self.context_name] = stack
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
```

### Test file reference: `tests/test_custom_middleware_exception.py`
Shows the pattern for custom ASGI middleware with send/receive wrapping:
```python
class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
        self.app = app
        self.max_content_size = max_content_size

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
```

### Test Patterns
- Tests use `from fastapi.testclient import TestClient`
- Module-level `client = TestClient(app)` instantiation
- Standard pytest assertions on `response.status_code`, `response.json()`, `response.headers`
- No `tests/test_apps/` directory exists - tests are at `tests/` top level
- pytest config: strict markers, strict config, warnings as errors, 20s timeout

### FastAPI `__init__.py`
Does NOT import from `fastapi.middleware` — middleware modules are accessed directly like `from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware`

## Requirements

Create `fastapi/middleware/rate_limit.py` implementing:

1. **`RateLimitMiddleware` class** following the raw ASGI middleware pattern (like AsyncExitStackMiddleware):
   - `__init__(self, app: ASGIApp, requests_per_minute: int = 60, burst_size: int = 10)`
   - `async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None`

2. **Token bucket algorithm** for rate limiting:
   - Per-client IP address tracking
   - `requests_per_minute` (default 60) controls refill rate
   - `burst_size` (default 10) controls max tokens in bucket
   - Tokens refill at `requests_per_minute / 60` tokens per second

3. **HTTP 429 response** when rate limited:
   - JSON body: `{"detail": "Rate limit exceeded", "retry_after": <seconds>}`
   - Content-Type: application/json

4. **Response headers on ALL responses**:
   - `X-RateLimit-Limit`: the requests_per_minute value
   - `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: remaining requests (tokens) in bucket
   - `X-RateLimit-Reset`: seconds until bucket refills

5. **Export** from `fastapi/middleware/__init__.py`

6. **Test app** at `tests/test_apps/test_rate_limit_app.py` (need to create `tests/test_apps/` directory with `__init__.py`)

7. All existing tests must still pass.

## Design Considerations

- The middleware must handle non-HTTP scopes (websocket, lifespan) by passing them through without rate limiting
- Client IP extraction from `scope["client"]` (tuple of (host, port))
- Need to handle case where `scope["client"]` is None
- Token bucket state must be stored in memory (dict of IP -> bucket)
- Need to intercept the `send` callable to inject headers into the response
- For 429 responses, must construct a full ASGI HTTP response manually (http.response.start + http.response.body)
- Consider thread safety if needed (asyncio is single-threaded per event loop, so dict access is safe)
- Consider memory: should we have a cleanup mechanism for stale IP entries?

Please provide a detailed step-by-step implementation plan covering:
1. The token bucket algorithm implementation details
2. The middleware class structure with exact method signatures
3. How to intercept and modify response headers (send wrapper pattern)
4. How to construct the 429 ASGI response
5. The test app structure and what test cases to include
6. The export from __init__.py
7. Edge cases to handle

- Need to handle case where `scope["client"]` is None
- Token bucket state must be stored in memory (dict of IP -> bucket)
- Need to intercept the `send` callable to inject headers into the response
- For 429 responses, must construct a full ASGI HTTP response manually (http.response.start + http.response.body)
- Consider thread safety if needed (asyncio is single-threaded per event loop, so dict access is safe)
- Consider memory: should we have a cleanup mechanism for stale IP entries?

Please provide a detailed step-by-step implementation plan covering:
1. The token bucket algorithm implementation details
2. The middleware class structure with exact method signatures
3. How to intercept and modify response headers (send wrapper pattern)
4. How to construct the 429 ASGI response
5. The test app structure and what test cases to include
6. The export from __init__.py
7. Edge cases to handle

12	class ContentSizeLimitMiddleware:
13	    """Content size limiting middleware for ASGI applications
14	    Args:
15	      app (ASGI application): ASGI application
16	      max_content_size (optional): the maximum content size allowed in bytes, None for no limit
17	    """
18	
19	    def __init__(self, app: APIRouter, max_content_size: int | None = None):
20	        self.app = app
21	        self.max_content_size = max_content_size
22	
23	    def receive_wrapper(self, receive):
24	        received = 0
25	
26	        async def inner():
27	            nonlocal received
28	            message = await receive()
29	            if message["type"] != "http.request":
30	                return message  # pragma: no cover
31	
32	            body_len = len(message.get("body", b""))
33	            received += body_len
34	            if received > self.max_content_size:
35	                raise HTTPException(
36	                    422,
37	                    detail={
38	                        "name": "ContentSizeLimitExceeded",
39	                        "code": 999,
40	                        "message": "File limit exceeded",
41	                    },
42	                )
43	            return message
44	
45	        return inner
46

48	        if scope["type"] != "http" or self.max_content_size is None:
49	            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
50	            return
51	
52	        wrapper = self.receive_wrapper(receive)
53	        await self.app(scope, wrapper, send)
